annotations.label: Distribution (generalized function)

anchor.snippet: are objects that generalize the classical notion of functions

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Distribution Ω F n` (notation `𝓓'(Ω, F)`) is the space of continuous linear functionals on test functions, the standard definition of distributions.

annotations.label: Tempered distributions vs distributions

anchor.snippet: Every tempered distribution is a distribution in the normal sense, but the converse is not true

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `TemperedDistribution` and `Distribution` separately but has no embedding of 𝓢' into 𝓓'.

annotations.label: Test function space D(R)

anchor.snippet: the set of test functions considered is D( R )

annotations.note: `TestFunction Ω F` (notation `𝓓(Ω, F)`) generalizes D(ℝ) to smooth compactly supported maps on any open set.

annotations.label: Distribution as linear mapping

anchor.snippet: A distribution T is a linear mapping T : D( R ) → R .

annotations.note: `Distribution` is an abbrev for continuous linear maps `𝓓(Ω, ℝ) →L_c[ℝ] F`, generalizing the linear map D(ℝ)→ℝ.

annotations.label: Dirac delta distribution

anchor.snippet: A simple example of a distribution is the Dirac delta δ , defined by

annotations.note: `Distribution.delta x` is defined with `delta_apply : delta x f = f x`, exactly the Dirac delta evaluation.

annotations.label: Distribution from locally integrable function

anchor.snippet: Suppose that f : R → R is a locally integrable function .

annotations.note: Integration of test functions against a locally integrable function is realized as a continuous linear map, but it is not packaged as the canonical embedding of locally integrable functions into 𝓓'.

annotations.label: Distribution from Radon measure

anchor.snippet: if μ is a Radon measure on R , then a corresponding distribution R μ may be defined by

annotations.note: Integrating test functions against a measure is available, and `Measure.toTemperedDistribution` exists for tempered distributions, but no general measure→𝓓' map is defined.

annotations.label: Point-mass measure gives Dirac delta

anchor.snippet: if P is the point-mass measure on R that assigns measure one to the singleton set {0}

annotations.note: `toTemperedDistribution_dirac_eq_delta : (dirac x).toTemperedDistribution = delta x` states exactly that the point-mass measure is the delta distribution.

annotations.label: Schwartz' Impossibility Theorem

anchor.snippet: is usually referred to as the Schwartz' Impossibility Theorem .

annotations.note: No formalization of Schwartz's impossibility theorem on multiplication of distributions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Derivative of a distribution

anchor.snippet: we should define its derivative

annotations.note: `lineDerivCLM_apply : lineDerivCLM v T f = -T (lineDerivCLM v f)` is the distributional derivative defined by transposing differentiation, generalizing the 1-D derivative.

annotations.label: Heaviside step function derivative is delta

anchor.snippet: It is the derivative of the distribution corresponding to the Heaviside step function H

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Heaviside step function nor the statement that its distributional derivative is δ.

annotations.label: Derivative of the Dirac delta

anchor.snippet: the derivative of the Dirac delta is the distribution defined by the equation

annotations.note: Both `Distribution.delta` and `lineDerivCLM` exist so δ′ is expressible as `lineDerivCLM v (delta x)`, but no dedicated lemma computes the derivative of the delta.

annotations.label: Test function space D(U)

anchor.snippet: The space D( U ) of test functions on U is defined as follows.

annotations.note: `TestFunction Ω F` is the space of smooth compactly supported maps with support in the open set Ω, i.e. D(U).

annotations.label: Compact support of a function

anchor.snippet: is said to have compact support if there exists a compact subset K of U

annotations.note: `HasCompactSupport f` (closure of the support is compact) is the standard Mathlib notion of compact support.

annotations.label: Convergence in D(U)

anchor.snippet: in D( U ) is said to converge to

annotations.note: The LF topology on `TestFunction` is defined, so convergence is the topological convergence, but the explicit sequential characterization (common compact support plus uniform convergence of all derivatives) is not stated.

annotations.label: D(U) is complete LCTVS with Heine–Borel property

anchor.snippet: D( U ) becomes a complete locally convex topological vector space satisfying the Heine–Borel property

annotations.note: The locally convex TVS structure on `TestFunction` is established, but completeness and the Heine–Borel (Montel) property are not proved.

annotations.label: Inductive limit (final) topology

anchor.snippet: the inductive limit topology, or final topology , τ on X is the finest locally convex vector space topology

annotations.note: `TestFunction.topologicalSpace` is built as the finest locally convex topology coarser than the inductive-limit (`originalTop`) topology, instantiating this construction for test functions, but there is no general abstract LF/final-topology API.

annotations.label: LF space

anchor.snippet: ( X , τ) is called an LF space .

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general notion of LF space (countable strict inductive limit of Fréchet spaces).

annotations.label: Completeness of (D(U), τ)

anchor.snippet: The completeness of ( D ( U ), τ) then follow from that of

annotations.note: No completeness instance or theorem for the test function space topology exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-metrizability of τ

anchor.snippet: The topology τ is not metrizable by the Baire category theorem

annotations.note: The non-metrizability of the test function space topology is not formalized.

annotations.label: Distribution on U

anchor.snippet: A distribution on U is a continuous linear functional T : D( U ) → R

annotations.note: `𝓓'(Ω, ℝ) = Distribution Ω ℝ ⊤` is exactly the space of continuous linear functionals on D(U).

annotations.label: Continuity criterion for distributions

anchor.snippet: T is continuous if and only if for every compact subset K of U there exists a positive constant

annotations.note: The universal property characterizes continuity by restriction to each compact K, but the explicit seminorm bound |T φ| ≤ C·sup‖∂^α φ‖ criterion is not stated.

annotations.label: Space of distributions D′(U)

anchor.snippet: The space of distributions on U is denoted by D′( U ) and it is the continuous dual space of D( U ).

annotations.note: `Distribution Ω ℝ ⊤` is the continuous dual of test functions, matching D′(U).

annotations.label: Duality pairing

anchor.snippet: The duality pairing between a distribution T in D′( U ) and a test function

annotations.note: The pairing ⟨T, φ⟩ is the `FunLike` application `T φ` of the continuous linear functional `T : 𝓓'(Ω, ℝ)`.

annotations.label: Weak-* convergence of distributions

anchor.snippet: A sequence of distributions ( T k ) converges with respect to the weak-* topology

annotations.note: 𝓓'(Ω, F) carries the compact-convergence topology, which agrees with weak-* on sequences (noted in the file), but there is no explicit weak-* convergence definition.

annotations.label: Sequence approximating Dirac delta

anchor.snippet: For example, if f k : R → R is the function

annotations.note: No theorem stating that a concrete sequence of functions converges to δ in the distributional sense is present.

annotations.label: Locally integrable function

anchor.snippet: is called locally integrable if it is Lebesgue integrable over every compact subset K of U .

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.LocallyIntegrable` (and `LocallyIntegrableOn`) is integrability on a neighborhood of each point / every compact set.

annotations.label: Equality of distributions iff equal a.e.

anchor.snippet: If f and g are two locally integrable functions, then the associated distributions T f and T g are equal

annotations.note: `ae_eq_of_integral_contDiff_smul_eq` proves that two locally integrable functions giving equal integrals against all test functions are equal a.e. (the nontrivial direction).

annotations.label: Schwartz representation by Radon measures

anchor.snippet: every distribution which is non-negative on non-negative functions is of this form for some (positive) Radon measure.

annotations.note: The Riesz–Markov–Kakutani theorem represents positive linear functionals on C_c(X) by measures, but the positive-distribution version is not stated.

annotations.label: Density of test functions in D′(U)

anchor.snippet: they are dense in D′( U )

annotations.note: Density of test functions inside the space of distributions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Extension of operators by limit

anchor.snippet: it is possible to extend A to a mapping

annotations.note: Extension of an operator on test functions to distributions is realized concretely via precomposition (`precompCompactConvergenceCLM`), but there is no general 'extension by continuity/transpose' theorem.

annotations.label: Transpose of an operator

anchor.snippet: is a continuous linear operator, then the transpose is an operator

annotations.note: Precomposing a distribution with an operator on test functions (`precompCompactConvergenceCLM`) implements the transpose, but no distribution-specific 'transpose' API is provided.

annotations.label: Partial derivative of a distribution

anchor.snippet: the partial derivative of T with respect to the coordinate x k is defined by the formula

annotations.note: `Distribution.lineDerivCLM v` is the directional derivative of a distribution; the partial ∂/∂x_k is the line derivative along the k-th basis vector.

annotations.label: Multi-index derivative of a distribution

anchor.snippet: More generally, if α = (α 1 , ..., α n ) is an arbitrary multi-index

annotations.note: `iteratedLineDerivOp` (notation `∂^{m}`) gives iterated line derivatives and applies to distributions via the `LineDeriv` instance, covering multi-index derivatives.

annotations.label: Differentiation is continuous on D′(U)

anchor.snippet: Differentiation of distributions is a continuous operator on D′( U )

annotations.note: `lineDerivCLM` is a bundled `→L[ℝ]` continuous linear map, so distributional differentiation is continuous by construction.

annotations.label: Product of smooth function and distribution

anchor.snippet: then the product m T is defined by

annotations.note: Multiplication by a temperate-growth function is defined for tempered distributions, but multiplication of a general smooth function with a distribution on 𝓓'(U) is not.

annotations.label: D′(U) as module over C∞(U)

anchor.snippet: D′( U ) is a module over the ring C ∞ ( U ).

annotations.note: The module structure of distributions over the ring of smooth functions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Identity mδ = m(0)δ

anchor.snippet: if δ is the Dirac delta distribution on R , then mδ = m (0) δ

annotations.note: Multiplication (`smulLeftCLM`) and `delta` both exist (with `smulLeftCLM_const`), but the specific identity mδ = m(0)δ is not a named lemma.

annotations.label: Linear differential operator on distributions

anchor.snippet: A linear differential operator P takes a distribution T ∈ D′( U ) to another distribution PT

annotations.note: There is no general notion of a linear differential operator acting on distributions, only specific operations (derivative, multiplication).

annotations.label: Order of a differential operator

anchor.snippet: is called the order of P .

annotations.note: The order of a differential operator is not formalized in this context.

annotations.label: Pullback of a distribution along a submersion

anchor.snippet: Then provided F is a submersion , it is possible to define

annotations.note: Pullback (composition) of a distribution along a submersion is not formalized.

annotations.label: Submersion equivalent to surjective Jacobian

anchor.snippet: The condition that F be a submersion is equivalent to the requirement that the Jacobian derivative

annotations.note: Mathlib has no `Submersion` definition characterized by surjective Jacobian (only passing mentions in the immersion file).

annotations.label: Pullback under diffeomorphism

anchor.snippet: In the special case when F is a diffeomorphism from an open subset V of R n

annotations.note: Pullback of distributions under a diffeomorphism is not formalized.

annotations.label: Distributions form a sheaf

anchor.snippet: Such a structure is known as a sheaf .

annotations.note: The sheaf structure of distributions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Restriction of a distribution

anchor.snippet: the restriction mapping ρ VU is defined to be the transpose of E VU .

annotations.note: No restriction map of distributions to a smaller open set is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Restriction not injective nor surjective

anchor.snippet: Unless U = V , the restriction to V is neither injective nor surjective .

annotations.note: Since the restriction map is not formalized, properties of its (non)injectivity/surjectivity are absent.

annotations.label: Vanishing of a distribution on an open set

anchor.snippet: T is said to vanish on an open set V of U if T lies in the kernel of the restriction map

annotations.note: `IsVanishingOn f s` states f kills every test function with support contained in s, the standard notion of vanishing on a set.

annotations.label: Support of a distribution

anchor.snippet: The support of T is the complement of V in U .

annotations.note: `dsupport f` is the intersection of closed sets on whose complement f vanishes, i.e. the distributional support.

annotations.label: Compactly supported distribution

anchor.snippet: The distribution T has compact support if its support is a compact set.

annotations.note: `dsupport` is defined so compact support is expressible as `IsCompact (dsupport T)`, but there is no dedicated compactly-supported-distribution definition.

annotations.label: Compactly supported distributions act on C∞(U)

anchor.snippet: Compactly supported distributions define continuous linear functionals on the space

annotations.note: The extension of compactly supported distributions to functionals on C∞(U) (the dual ℰ′) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Space of tempered distributions

anchor.snippet: one can define the space of tempered distributions S′( R n ), a subspace of D′( R n ).

annotations.note: `TemperedDistribution E F` (notation `𝓢'(E, F)`) is the space of tempered distributions, though its identification as a subspace of 𝓓' is not formalized.

annotations.label: Schwartz space

anchor.snippet: the so-called Schwartz space S ( R n ), is the function space of all infinitely differentiable functions that are rapidly decreasing

annotations.note: `SchwartzMap E F` is the space of smooth functions all of whose derivatives decay faster than any power of ‖x‖.

annotations.label: Schwartz function via seminorms

anchor.snippet: is a Schwartz function if all the values satisfy

annotations.note: `SchwartzMap.seminorm` gives the family of seminorms ‖x‖^k‖iteratedFDeriv n f‖, equivalent to the x^α∂^β characterization.

annotations.label: Schwartz space metrizable and complete

anchor.snippet: The Schwartz space is metrizable and complete .

annotations.note: Schwartz space is shown to be a first-countable locally convex TVS (hence metrizable), but completeness is not established as an instance/theorem.

annotations.label: Fourier transform preserves Schwartz space

anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform of a Schwartz function is also a Schwartz function.

annotations.note: `fourierTransformCLM : 𝓢(V, E) →L[𝕜] 𝓢(V, E)` shows the Fourier transform maps Schwartz space to itself continuously.

annotations.label: Tempered distribution as dual of Schwartz space

anchor.snippet: The space of tempered distributions is defined as the (continuous) dual of the Schwartz space.

annotations.note: `TemperedDistribution E F` is defined as `𝓢(E, ℂ) →Lₚₜ[ℂ] F`, the (pointwise-topology) continuous dual of Schwartz space.

annotations.label: Derivative of tempered distribution is tempered

anchor.snippet: The derivative of a tempered distribution is again a tempered distribution.

annotations.note: `derivCLM : 𝓢'(ℝ, F) →L[ℂ] 𝓢'(ℝ, F)` (and the `LineDeriv` instance) maps tempered distributions to tempered distributions.

annotations.label: Examples of tempered distributions

anchor.snippet: Tempered distributions generalize the bounded (or slow-growing) locally integrable functions

annotations.note: `HasTemperateGrowth.toTemperedDistribution` (and `Lp.toTemperedDistribution`) realize slow-growing/Lp functions as tempered distributions.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of a tempered distribution

anchor.snippet: we can define the Fourier transform of the tempered distribution T by

annotations.note: The `FourierTransform` instance defines 𝓕 on 𝓢' by `fourier_apply : 𝓕 f g = f (𝓕 g)`, the transpose definition.

annotations.label: Fourier transform is a bijection on S′

anchor.snippet: The Fourier transform is a continuous, linear, bijective operator from the space of tempered distributions to itself.

annotations.note: The `FourierPair`/`FourierInvPair` instances for 𝓢' make `fourierCLE ℂ 𝓢'(E,F)` a continuous linear equivalence, i.e. a continuous linear bijection of tempered distributions.

annotations.label: Fourier transform of constant 1 is delta

anchor.snippet: the Fourier transform of the constant function equal to 1 is the δ distribution.

annotations.note: `fourier_delta_zero` proves 𝓕 δ = volume (i.e. 𝓕 δ = 1); the converse 𝓕 1 = δ is not stated directly (it follows by inversion).

annotations.label: Convolution operator with test function

anchor.snippet: If f ∈ D( R n ) is a compactly supported smooth test function, then convolution with f

annotations.note: Convolution of functions `f ⋆[L, μ] g` is defined, but convolution as an operator on test-function/distribution spaces is not.

annotations.label: Convolution of function with distribution

anchor.snippet: Convolution of f with a distribution T ∈ D′( R n ) can be defined by taking the transpose

annotations.note: Convolution of a function with a distribution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convolution via translation operator

anchor.snippet: An alternative way to define the convolution of a function f and a distribution T is to use the translation operator

annotations.note: The translation-operator definition of convolution with a distribution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convolution with smooth function is smooth

anchor.snippet: the convolution of a smooth, compactly supported function and a distribution is a smooth function.

annotations.note: Smoothness of convolution of a compactly supported smooth function with a (locally integrable) function is proved, but the distributional version is not.

annotations.label: Titchmarsh convolution theorem (support)

anchor.snippet: the Titchmarsh convolution theorem ( Hörmander 1983 , Theorem 4.3.3) implies that

annotations.note: The Titchmarsh convolution theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convolution of two distributions

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to define the convolution of two distributions S and T on R n , provided one of them has compact support.

annotations.note: Convolution of two distributions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Explicit characterization of convolution

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to provide a more explicit characterization of the convolution of distributions

annotations.note: No explicit characterization of distribution convolution exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distributions are locally derivatives of continuous functions

anchor.section: Distributions as derivatives of continuous functions
anchor.snippet: any distribution is locally a (multiple) derivative of a continuous function.

annotations.note: The local structure theorem (distributions are local derivatives of continuous functions) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Structure of tempered distributions

anchor.snippet: If f ∈ S ′( R n ) is a tempered distribution, then there exists a constant C > 0

annotations.note: The structure theorem for tempered distributions (as derivatives of bounded continuous functions) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Restriction to compact sets as derivative

anchor.section: Restriction of distributions to compact sets
anchor.snippet: If f ∈ D′( R n ), then for any compact set K ⊂ R n , there exists a continuous function F

annotations.note: This structure result on compact sets is not formalized.

annotations.label: Distributions with point support

anchor.snippet: If f has support at a single point { P }, then f is in fact a finite linear combination of distributional derivatives

annotations.note: The theorem characterizing distributions supported at a point as finite combinations of derivatives of δ is not formalized.

annotations.label: Local structure of general distributions

anchor.snippet: A version of the above theorem holds locally in the following sense

annotations.note: The general local structure theorem for distributions is not formalized.

annotations.label: No associative product of distributions

anchor.snippet: there is no associative product of two distributions extending the product

annotations.note: The impossibility of an associative product of distributions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Non-associativity with p.v. 1/x and delta

anchor.snippet: if p.v. 1/ x is the distribution obtained by the Cauchy principal value

annotations.note: Neither the principal-value distribution p.v. 1/x nor the non-associativity example is in Mathlib.
